military_service.unit: Maj. Elizur Talcott of Glastonbury — Troop of horses
military_service.notes: At age 31 enlisted in Maj. Elizur Talcott of Glastonbury's Troop of horses for service in the alarm for the relief of Fort William Henry and parts adjacent, August 1757 — 14 days of service. Enlisted approximately two months after his nephew Martin Hooker (H00410) was killed June 10, 1757 in the same war. The connection to Maj. Elizur Talcott (T00459, gen 3 from John Talcott) is a Hooker↔Talcott cross-tree military link.

military_service.unit: Capt. Nathaniel Chapman's Company, Lt-Col Joseph Baldwin's Regiment, Col. Mead's drafted men
military_service.notes: Served under Capt. Nathaniel Chapman in Lt-Col Joseph Baldwin's Regiment at Fishkill, NY. DAR Ancestor #A057686 (Connecticut, Private). Source: Record of Service of Connecticut Men in the War of the Revolution (1889), p. 522.

narrative_blocks.body: Samuel served as Private in two of the colonial era's military campaigns twenty years apart. In August 1757 at age 31 he joined Maj. Elizur Talcott's Troop of horses for fourteen days during the Fort William Henry alarm. Twenty years later at age 51 he served again from December 1777 to March 1778 in Capt. Nathaniel Chapman's Company at Fishkill NY — earning DAR ancestor recognition #A057686.

narrative_blocks.header: Enlisted weeks after his nephew Martin's death
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel's nephew Martin Hooker — son of Samuel's brother Andrew — was killed in the same war on June 10, 1757 at the deadly sentry post documented in Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy. About two months later in August 1757, Samuel Hooker Jr enlisted at age 31 in Maj. Elizur Talcott's Troop of horses for the Fort William Henry alarm. Whether the enlistment was motivated by news of Martin's death is undocumented but the timing is suggestive.

cross_connections.display_label: His son Dr. William married my daughter Amelia in 1814.

sources.short: Record of Service of Connecticut Men in the War of the Revolution, 1889
Berlin-Peck: Revolutionary War Soldiers of the Town of Berlin

cross_connections.display_label: His son Dr. William married my daughter Amelia in 1814.
commanded the company he guarded Boston with in early 1776

military_service.unit: Col. Erastus Wolcott's Regt. (Capt. Selah Hart's 3rd Co.); Gen. Wadsworth's Brigade; Maj. Sheldon's Light Horse (Capt. Daniel Sloper's Co.)
military_service.notes: 1776 campaign across three commands. Winter 1776: guarded besieged Boston about six weeks in Capt. Selah Hart's 3rd Company, Col. Erastus Wolcott's Regiment. Summer 1776: present at the Battle of Long Island (Aug 27) with Gen. James Wadsworth's Brigade and the retreat from New York. Nov 4–Dec 24, 1776: Corporal in Capt. Daniel Sloper's Company, Maj. Sheldon's Regiment of Light Horse, on Washington's retreat through New Jersey. Source: Record of Service of Connecticut Men in the War of the Revolution (1889), pp. 381, 392, 475; Berlin-Peck, Revolutionary War Soldiers of the Town of Berlin.

narrative_blocks.header: Married three times, his last two wives widows
narrative_blocks.body: After Ann Hooker died in 1808 he married twice more, both times to widows: Sabra Winchell Stanley, and in 1822 Abigail Hart Smith, herself already twice-widowed. His will, presented in court July 1827, left the farm and stock to his son Seth and the remainder to his daughters Lydia, Nancy, and Amelia.

narrative_blocks.header: His whole war fit in one brutal year
narrative_blocks.body: Seth's Revolutionary service was crowded into 1776. That year he helped guard besieged Boston through the winter, stood with the army in its rout at the Battle of Long Island, and rode in the light horse covering Washington's retreat across New Jersey.

narrative_blocks.header: Left Connecticut and died wealthy in New York
narrative_blocks.body: He married Sally Fenn and removed to New York State, where according to the family genealogy he died a rich man.

narrative_blocks.header: Joined the Stanley family's migration to Ohio
narrative_blocks.body: She and her husband Sylvester Hurlbut removed from Connecticut to Portage County, Ohio, part of the broader Stanley and Hurlbut migration westward. Both are buried at Drakesburg in Freedom Township.

sources.short: Find a Grave — South Dover Rural Cemetery

narrative_blocks.header: Cousin marriage united two Hooker branches
narrative_blocks.body: Amelia carried Hooker blood from both parents — her mother Ann Hooker Stanley descended from Samuel Hooker Jr., her father Seth Stanley descended through his mother Hannah Hart from Abigail Hooker. When she married her cousin Dr. William Hooker in Hartford on January 9, 1814, three Hooker branches converged in their two children — a triple descent that closed a century-and-a-half family loop.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed at thirty-nine with eight children
narrative_blocks.body: Esther was thirty-nine years old when her husband John House IV died in November 1798, leaving her with eight children — the youngest Cyprian still an infant of about eighteen months. She never remarried and lived another fifty years, dying in East Hartford in 1848 at age eighty-nine.

sources.short: Find a Grave — Old Eastbury Cemetery

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his father John House IV — eldest son carrying the patrilineal name forward.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost three young children including two in 1817
narrative_blocks.body: Betsey lost three of her six children before they reached age five — Walter and Orange S. died together in 1817 (ages four and three, suggesting an epidemic or shared illness), and her infant Sylvester died in 1829. Of her three surviving children — Roxanna, Sidney, and Edwin — all reached old age.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost at seven between siblings' births
narrative_blocks.body: Roxana was Esther's fourth child, born 1787 between Clarissa (1786) and Sophronia (1788). She died in 1794 at age seven — four years before her father's death and three years before her youngest brother Cyprian was born. She is the only documented child Esther lost in childhood.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost his father as an infant of one
narrative_blocks.body: Cyprian was Esther's eighth and youngest child, born April 5, 1797 in Glastonbury Connecticut. His father John House IV died on November 14, 1798 when Cyprian was only nineteen months old. Cyprian later migrated north to Turin Lewis County New York, married Sophia Risley in 1819, raised four children there, and lived to age eighty-six.

sources.short: Find a Grave — Christian Lane Cemetery

cross_connections.display_label: Aunt and mother-in-law via her son's 1807 cousin marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her first cousin in late life
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker married Hooker Gilbert on June 30, 1807 in Kensington when she was 44 — her only documented marriage. Hooker Gilbert was her maternal first cousin: her father Samuel Hooker Jr. and his mother Elizabeth Hooker Gilbert were siblings. The couple lived together for thirty-three years without documented children; Sarah died December 4, 1840, and Hooker Gilbert followed two days later.

sources.short: Find a Grave — Christian Lane Cemetery

cross_connections.display_label: Maternal uncle whose daughter Sarah he married in 1807.

naming_inspiration.description: Given the surname 'Hooker' as his first name in honor of his mother Elizabeth Hooker Gilbert's family. First-name use of the maternal surname is unusual and emphatic — he later married into the Hooker line as well, his second wife Sarah Hooker (H00432) being his maternal first cousin.

military_service.unit: Multiple — Capt. Gad Stanley's Co (1776), Capt. Lankton's Co under Col. Noadiah Hooker's Regt (1777), Capt. Matthew Cole's Co (1779)
military_service.notes: Three documented enlistments: (1) June 1776 — four months in Capt. Gad Stanley's Company at the Battle of Harlem Heights; (2) summer 1777 — three months in Capt. Lankton's Company under Col. Noadiah Hooker's Regiment; (3) July 1779 — ten days in Capt. Matthew Cole's Company at the Battle of New Haven (Tryon's Raid). Placed on the federal pension list in 1838. DAR Ancestor #A045110. Source: Berlin-Peck Memorial Library.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Revolutionary War enlistments and 1838 pension
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker Gilbert enlisted three times during the Revolution: four months in June 1776 in Capt. Gad Stanley's Company at the Battle of Harlem Heights; three months in summer 1777 in Capt. Lankton's Company under Col. Noadiah Hooker's Regiment; and ten days in July 1779 in Capt. Matthew Cole's Company at the Battle of New Haven during Tryon's Raid. He was placed on the federal pension list in 1838 and is recorded as DAR Ancestor #A045110.

narrative_blocks.header: Married his first cousin Sarah Hooker in 1807
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker Gilbert's first wife Candace Sage died May 15, 1805. Two years later on June 30, 1807 he married Sarah Hooker — his maternal first cousin and the daughter of his uncle Samuel Hooker Jr. The marriage united two grandchildren of Samuel Hooker via his daughter Elizabeth Hooker Gilbert and his son Samuel Hooker Jr.

narrative_blocks.header: Both spouses died within two days December 1840
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker died December 4, 1840 at Kensington Connecticut at age 77. Hooker Gilbert followed her two days later on December 6, 1840 at age 86 — together they had been married for thirty-three years.

narrative_blocks.header: Two grandchildren of Samuel Hooker reunited 1807
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker Gilbert and Sarah Hooker were the marriage point where two branches of the Hooker line came back together. His mother Elizabeth and her father Samuel Jr. had been siblings — both children of Samuel Hooker of Kensington. The cousin marriage produced no documented children of its own but closed a fifty-year gap between the two branches.

burialCemetery.name: Christian Lane Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Kensington is a parish of Berlin, CT — sources may list burial location as either 'Kensington' or 'Berlin'. John Hooker No. 2 (H00098) and Mercy Hart Hooker (I00041) buried here. Burial site of Samuel Hooker (H00086) and family
